description: Early archaeological cultures and technologies, including Soi Nhụ, Ngườm, Sơn Vi, Hoabinhian, Bắc Sơn, Quỳnh Văn, Cái Bèo, early rice cultivation, and Đa Bút.
Traditional account of Kinh Dương Vương unifying vassal states into Xích Quỷ and ruling as Hùng king from Phong Châu.
Traditional Hùng king period associated with Lạc Việt society, wet-rice agriculture, calendar use, bronze casting, irrigation, Đông Sơn culture, and early Vietnamese identity.
date_status: Legendary/traditional dates; cultural developments vary by source

description: Thục Phán/An Dương Vương conquered Văn Lang, renamed the country Âu Lạc, and ruled from Cổ Loa Citadel until Zhao Tuo captured Cổ Loa.

main_capital: Panyu, Guangzhou; Jiaozhi/Jiuzhen in Vietnam region
description: Zhao Tuo took control of Âu Lạc territory, organized Jiaozhi and Jiuzhen, and later declared the kingdom of Nanyue before Han conquest.
date_status: Historical dates from timeline; verify details

description: Han conquest of Nanyue brought Jiaozhi and related commanderies under Chinese imperial administration until the Trưng Sisters' uprising.
The Trưng Sisters led a major rebellion against Han authority in the Red River Delta before Han forces restored control.
Chinese imperial administration resumed after the Trưng Sisters' defeat and lasted until Lý Nam Đế established Vạn Xuân.

period_name: Vạn Xuân / Early Lý and Triệu Việt Vương
description: Lý Nam Đế proclaimed Vạn Xuân after expelling Liang forces; later rule involved Triệu Việt Vương, Lý Thiên Bảo, and Hậu Lý Nam Đế before Sui conquest.
Sui and later Tang-era control over the region, with notable resistance movements such as Mai Thúc Loan and Phùng Hưng before Ngô Quyền's victory.

period_name: Ngô dynasty and Twelve Warlords transition
description: Ngô Quyền's victory at Bạch Đằng ended long Chinese domination and founded the Ngô dynasty, followed by the Anarchy of the Twelve Warlords.
Đinh Bộ Lĩnh unified the country after the warlord period and established Đại Cồ Việt with capital at Hoa Lư.
Lê Hoàn took power, defended Đại Cồ Việt against Song invasion, and campaigned against Champa before the Lý dynasty began.
Lý Thái Tổ founded the dynasty and moved the capital from Hoa Lư to Đại La/Thăng Long; the period saw state consolidation, exams, education, and conflict with Song.
The Trần dynasty ruled Đại Việt and is strongly associated with resistance against Mongol/Yuan invasions, the Bình Than and Diên Hồng conferences, and Trần Hưng Đạo.

main_capital: Tây Đô / Citadel of the Hồ dynasty
description: Hồ Quý Ly overthrew the Trần, renamed the country Đại Ngu, moved the capital, and faced the Ming–Hồ War.

period_name: Ming occupation and Lam Sơn uprising
main_capital: Đông Quan; Lam Sơn resistance base
description: Ming forces captured Đại Ngu capitals; Lê Lợi's Lam Sơn uprising restored independence and the Bình Ngô đại cáo affirmed Đại Việt's sovereignty.

period_name: Later Lê dynasty / early consolidated Lê
description: Lê Lợi founded the Later Lê dynasty; under rulers such as Lê Thánh Tông, Đại Việt developed official histories, law codes, and centralized institutions.

main_capital: Đông Kinh; Cao Bằng after retreat
description: Mạc Đăng Dung forced Lê Cung Hoàng to abdicate and established the Mạc dynasty; the Lê–Mạc War ended with the Revival Lê regaining the capital.

main_capital: Đông Kinh/Thăng Long; Phú Xuân/Gia Định in the south
description: The Revival Lê restored the dynasty while real power was divided between Trịnh lords in the north and Nguyễn lords in the south, including the Trịnh–Nguyễn War from 1627.
The Tây Sơn forces overthrew old power centers, fought Siamese and Qing forces, and ruled before Nguyễn Ánh/Gia Long defeated the final Tây Sơn forces.
Gia Long became ruler, moved the capital to Huế, and the Nguyễn state developed law, literature, and governance before French military intervention.

period_name: French conquest and early colonial takeover
description: French military campaigns, the Treaty of Saigon, the establishment of Cochinchina, and the crisis around Huế transformed Vietnam's sovereignty.
Colonial rule shaped politics, economy, education, and resistance movements, including Đông Du, Duy Tân, the rise of nationalism, CPV founding in 1930, and the Empire of Vietnam in 1945.

period_name: August Revolution and First Indochina War
description: The August Revolution, Bảo Đại's abdication, postwar conflict, State of Vietnam, Battle of Điện Biên Phủ, Geneva Conference, and the end of the First Indochina War.

period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
description: Geneva arrangements produced North and South Vietnam; the period includes the Republic of Vietnam, Vietnam War escalation, Tet Offensive, Paris Peace Accords, and the Fall of Saigon.

period_name: Reunified Socialist Republic and postwar conflicts
description: Vietnam was formally unified as the Socialist Republic of Vietnam, joined the United Nations, entered Comecon, fought the Khmer Rouge, and faced the Sino-Vietnamese War.
The Sixth National Party Congress launched Đổi Mới, followed by major reforms, changing external relations, ASEAN admission, and preparation for deeper global integration.
Contemporary Vietnam includes WTO admission, later Party Congresses, constitutional changes, maritime tensions, COVID-19, major scandals, leadership changes, and administrative restructuring.
date_status: Recent entries need external verification before publication
